# `eon prune` removes jobs that won't fire (done one-shots and
# disabled jobs). Default is dry-run; --force performs the delete.

# Seed: 1 enabled cron, 1 disabled cron, 1 enabled oneshot (future),
# 1 done oneshot (via daemon fire).
exec eon add --cron '@hourly' --name live -- /bin/echo hi
exec eon add --cron '@hourly' --name offcron -- /bin/echo off
exec eon disable offcron
exec eon add --at '+1h' --name pending -- /bin/echo pending
exec eon add --at '+1s' --name once -- /bin/echo done
exec eon daemon &daemon&
exec sleep 2.5
exec eon show once --json
stdout '"status": "done"'
exec eon stop
wait daemon

# Dry-run lists exactly the disabled and done jobs.
exec eon prune
stdout 'would prune 2'
stdout 'offcron'
stdout 'once'
! stdout '  live'
! stdout 'pending'
stdout 'dry run; nothing was modified'
stdout 'Re-run with --force'

# Nothing was actually deleted.
exec eon ls --all --json
stdout '"name": "offcron"'
stdout '"name": "once"'

# Status filter narrows the scope. With --status done we only see
# the done one-shot.
exec eon prune --status done
stdout 'would prune 1'
stdout 'once'
! stdout 'offcron'

# Status enabled (live + pending) is what the user would NOT
# normally prune; the filter still works because we trust them.
exec eon prune --status enabled
stdout 'would prune 2'
stdout 'live'
stdout 'pending'

# Kind narrows further: --kind cron restricts to cron jobs. With
# the default disabled+done filter, only the disabled cron matches.
exec eon prune --kind cron
stdout 'would prune 1'
stdout 'offcron'
! stdout 'once'

# JSON dry-run emits a JSON array of candidate jobs.
exec eon prune --json
stdout '"name": "offcron"'
stdout '"name": "once"'

# JSON --force emits the array of deleted IDs (5-char alphanumeric).
exec eon prune --force --json
stdout '"[0-9A-Za-z]{5}",'
stdout '"[0-9A-Za-z]{5}"'
exec eon ls --all --json
! stdout 'offcron'
! stdout '"name": "once"'
stdout '"name": "live"'
stdout '"name": "pending"'

# Second prune finds nothing to do.
exec eon prune
stdout 'nothing to prune'

# Bad filter values → usage error.
! exec eon prune --kind weird
stderr 'Usage error.*--kind'
! exec eon prune --status weird
stderr 'Usage error.*--status'

# No positional arguments accepted.
! exec eon prune extra
